# Diverís Vega López (English and Writing)

<!-- vimeo: 1111504073 | track: English (auto-generated) -->

What if AI could make learning a second language less intimidating and more personal for students? Hello, I am D Lopez and I use Chachi BD to help university students in Puerto Rico build confidence, clarity, and their own voice in English. Here's what I've built, how I did it and the impact it's having. As a professor in Puerto Rico, I teach first and second year English at a private university, working primarily with undergraduate students. I've also taught public speaking writing for the professional world and other English communication courses. My focus is communication that works. Regenerative AI used to support learning, not replace it. In practice, my classrooms include a broad range of English proficiency. Some students are bilingual or highly fluent, often through families, school or media exposure, while others have a limited day-to-day contact with English. These differences often reflect diverse schooling experiences and socioeconomic backgrounds, so instruction needs to be flexible, equitable, and confidence building to meet a wide range of proficiency levels while keeping voice and integrity central. I use two approaches and one custom tool, a feedback loop for writing live conversation, practice using chat, GT's, voice feature, and a custom GPT tutor I built called Hello English. In writing for the professional world, we used a feedback loop method. Students first drafted a cover letter, a resume and text for a mock professional website. They were then instructed to submit each draft to chat GPT for targeted feedback using a provided prompt in class. We reviewed the notes together, identified what was useful and why, and students revised and edited accordingly. Then they requested an improved version of their draft. That applies the critique. Finally, we compared the original and the AI revised version. Students highlight what matches their intent and what feels too generic. That contrast helped them hear their own voice and refine it. Here's an example of what can be used in a similar class scenario. Act as a hiring manager. Evaluate my cover letter, resume site copy for clarity, tone, and relevance to role industry. Gimme detailed feedback including five strengths and five specific improvements. With examples. Have students share their results in a group discussion. What was suggested? Did they agree or disagree with the feedback? What surprised them? After the first discussion, follow up with a second prompt. Exercise like this. Produce a revised version that keeps my details and voice and add a one sentence rationale per paragraph. Discuss once again the differences between the original and the AI generated version. What do they like or dislike? What makes it the text sound generic? What makes it sound human? What personal details or unique points would they add? These questions keep the conversation going. Now the class ends with personalizing students. Add brief anecdotes, concrete outcomes or details only they can provide. AI can support structure and clarity, but lived experiences give writing its voice for engagement and speaking practice I project or share on screen chat GBT with voice enabled students ask real questions and respond to follow-ups, practicing spontaneity and repair strategies. When I first introduced this exercise, students were surprised to learn about the voice feature and were very curious to how it worked and responded. I demonstrated what they can do with this feature and eventually many students chimed in with their own questions. Students who are shy with English, I encourage them to speak in Spanish and ask chat BT to reply in English. This help them to listen and interpret the answer in their own process and perspective. For conversation practice, you can use this prompt be my conversation partner. Topic, internships, ask one question at a time. If my answer is short, politely, push me to elaborate at the end, give me three pronunciation or phrasing tips and two expressions to practice. This type of prompt encourages students to practice and explore on their own time and pace. Additionally, this task can be assigned and students can share or copy their conversations for assessment. When the feature of creating your own GPTs became available, I played around with creating an embedding small task specific helpers in our learning management system and included links to the GPT. Inside modules or assignments, students could request rubric, align checks, structure, examples, or vocabulary practice. Every helper nudged them to think and revise, not outsource the task. After a while, I wanted to sort all of this into one single GPT for simplicity and quick help. So I built Hello English as a friendly practice tutor that holds voice conversations, breaks down grammar with plain examples, clarifies literature, passages, and guides through the writing process. Instead of doing it for the student, if a student asks for a full essay hello in class, redirects to an outline, asks questions that extract personal experiences and makes drafting checkpoints. Students write while the GPT coaches to build Hello ing glass. I started by defining its role as a supportive ESL tutor for Puerto Rican undergraduates. I wrote clear instructions so it would coach process, not ghost, right, asking one question at a time, prompting students for personal examples and redirecting full essay requests into outlines, checkpoints, and reflection. I also enabled optional Spanish support on request added conversation starters and included quick grammar explanations with short practice. Finally, I embedded the link in our learning management system so students could access it alongside the rubric. Some of the instructions I included in the GPT were be a friendly ESL tutor for first and second year undergraduates give one question at a time, keeping voice friendly pacing, never write full assignments, guide with an outline and checkpoints. Ask for personal details or examples and protect the student's voice. Also offer Spanish support on request clarifying terms with plain examples and with a quick practice task or two reflection questions. When applying these exercises, methods and activities, students report feeling less shy, speaking English and say the voice demo is a game changer. Having a tool they can use on their own helps with their confidence and growth and it shows later in class. These are students who participate more and express freely how much they liked the dynamic. Many also tell me that grammar finally clicks when chat. GBT explains its step by step, step-by-step. Most importantly, they can see the difference between AI output and writing. That carries their personal story and they choose the latter. Little by little, their language learning world improves with the help of chat GBT because tools like this are not here to replace, but to enhance their experience to ensure that the courses flow smoothly. Integrating chat GBTI set clear rules, disclose AI assistance when required, verify facts, respect, privacy, and never submit AI generated work as your own. Reflection is non-negotiable. Reflection demonstrates learning and the human process is just as or more powerful as the technological one to get started using chat GT for language learning. Begin with short, manageable tasks that you can demonstrate and the students can follow later. Try one assignment using chat BT only for feedback. Follow with a class discussion on comparison and experience, and then add a short reflection. Talk about what they kept, what they changed, and what they learned. Remember to always keep students' voices at the center of the lesson. Remember, with thoughtful design chat, GBT can become a bridge to learning. Thank you for watching my video. I am Professor Dire Vega Lopez, and if you teach English as a second or foreign language, feel free to adapt any of these ideas, prompts, and structures.
